---
title: "3.3.3 Magnetic, optical and solid-state storage"
subtitle: "Hard disk drives (HDD) · Hardware P123-P124"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        .reveal .term { color: #cf1f2e; font-weight: 700; }
        .reveal .mark { background: #fff36d; padding: 0 0.08em; }
        .reveal .tight-list li { margin-bottom: 0.24em; }
        .reveal .figure-note { font-size: 0.82em; line-height: 1.28; }
        .reveal .clean-table table { font-size: 0.76em; line-height: 1.1; }
        .reveal .clean-table th, .reveal .clean-table td { padding: 0.24em 0.32em; vertical-align: top; }
        </style>
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 3 Directory / 返回 Unit 3 目录](../../index.html)

## 3.3.3 Magnetic storage

### Hard disk drives (HDD)

Secondary (and off-line) storage falls into three categories according to the technology used:

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="term">magnetic</span> (磁性)
- <span class="term">solid state</span> (固态)
- <span class="term">optical</span> (光存储)
:::

<span class="term">Hard disk drives (HDD)</span> are still one of the most common methods used to store data on a computer.

## Figure 3.62

### HDD

![Figure 3.62 HDD](../../assets/3-hardware/3-3-3-magnetic-storage-hdd-p123-p124/Figure 3.62 HDD.png){fig-align="center" width="72%"}

## HDD Structure

### Platters and read-write heads

Data is stored in a digital format on the magnetic surfaces of the disks (or <span class="term">platters</span>).

::: {.tight-list}
- the hard disk drive will have a number of platters that can <span class="mark">spin at about 7000 times a second</span>
- <span class="term">read-write heads</span> (读写磁头) consist of electromagnets that are used to read data from or write data to the platters
- platters can be made from <span class="mark">aluminium</span>, <span class="mark">glass</span> or a <span class="mark">ceramic material</span>
- a number of read-write heads can access all of the surfaces of the platters in the disk drive
- normally each platter will have <span class="mark">two surfaces</span> which can be used to store data
:::

## Figure 3.63

### Tracks and sectors

![Figure 3.63 Tracks and sectors](../../assets/3-hardware/3-3-3-magnetic-storage-hdd-p123-p124/Figure 3.63 Tracks and sectors.png){fig-align="center" width="78%"}

These read-write heads can move very quickly. Typically, they can move from the <span class="mark">centre of the disk to the edge of the disk</span> (and back again) 50 times a second.

Data is stored on the surface in <span class="term">sectors</span> (扇区) and <span class="term">tracks</span> (磁道). A sector on a given track will contain a <span class="mark">fixed number of bytes</span>.

## Latency

### Why access is slow

Unfortunately, hard disk drives have very slow data access when compared to, for example, RAM.

Many applications require the read-write heads to constantly look for the correct blocks of data; this means a large number of head movements.

<span class="term">Latency</span> (延迟) is defined as the time it takes for a specific block of data on a data track to <span class="mark">rotate around to the read-write head</span>.

Users will sometimes notice the effect of latency when they see messages such as <span class="mark">'Please wait'</span> or, at its worst, <span class="mark">'not responding'</span>.

## Fragmentation and Removable HDDs

### Performance and portability

When a file or data is stored on a HDD, the required number of sectors needed to store the data will be allocated. However, the sectors allocated may not be <span class="mark">adjacent</span> to each other.

Through time, the HDD will undergo numerous deletions and editing which leads to sectors becoming increasingly <span class="term">fragmented</span> (碎片化), resulting in a gradual deterioration of HDD performance.

<span class="term">Defragmentation software</span> (磁盘碎片整理软件) can improve this situation by <span class="mark">'tidying up'</span> the disk sectors.

All data in a given sector on a HDD will be read <span class="mark">in order</span> (that is, sequentially); however, access to the sector itself will be by a <span class="mark">direct read-write head movement</span>.

Removable hard disk drives are essentially HDDs external to the computer that can be connected to the computer using one of the <span class="term">USB</span> ports.

## Classroom Check

### Keep the HDD description faithful

A complete answer should include:

::: {.tight-list}
- that HDDs are a form of <span class="term">magnetic storage</span>
- that data is stored on magnetic <span class="mark">platters</span> and accessed by <span class="mark">read-write heads</span>
- that data is organised in <span class="mark">tracks</span> and <span class="mark">sectors</span>
- that <span class="term">latency</span> is the time taken for a data block to rotate round to the read-write head
- that <span class="term">fragmentation</span> slows access because sectors may not be adjacent
- that removable HDDs can be connected using a <span class="term">USB</span> port
:::

## Bridge

### Next: solid-state storage

The next decks move from <span class="term">magnetic storage</span> to <span class="term">solid-state storage</span>, including <span class="term">SSD</span> and <span class="term">flash memory</span>.

## End

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 3 Directory / 返回 Unit 3 目录](../../index.html)
